Urges Myanmar to immediately commit the repatriation of Bangladesh and the voluntary and sustainable reintegration, in safety and dignity, of all Rohingya Muslims and members of other forcibly displaced minorities, recalling in this regard the bilateral arrangement between Bangladesh and Myanmar in November 2017 concerning the return of internally displaced persons from Rakhine and those residing in other host States, including through full cooperation with the Government of Bangladesh and the United Nations, in particular the Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees and, where appropriate, the Humanitarian Assistance Coordination Centre for Disaster Management of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, by guaranteeing to returnees freedom of movement and unhindered access to livelihoods and social services, including health services, education and housing, and compensation for all losses suffered;

30. Invites the United Nations, and calls upon other international bodies to provide the Government of Bangladesh and Myanmar with all necessary support to accelerate the voluntary and sustainable return, in safety and dignity, of Rohingya Muslims and members of other forcibly displaced minorities in Myanmar, including internally displaced persons;

31. Expresses its deep concern at the continuing restrictions on humanitarian access, in particular in the Rakhine, Chin, Kachin, Shan, Kayah and Kaitin States, and calls upon Myanmar to ensure full respect for international humanitarian law and to provide humanitarian personnel with safe and unhindered access to all parts of the country, to provide humanitarian assistance, including age and gender-sensitive assistance, and to provide supplies and equipment to enable them to carry out effectively their assistance to the civilian populations affected, including internally displaced persons, and calls upon Myanmar to enable members of the diplomatic corps, independent observers and representatives of the independent national and international media to access the country without fear of reprisals;

32. Expresses its concern at the continuing irregular maritime movements of Rohingya Muslims, which risk their lives by subjecting themselves to dangerous conditions in the hands of smugglers and traffickers of human beings who exploit them, which highlights their desperate situation and the urgent need to address the root causes of the suffering they endure, and calls upon the international community to take effective measures to stop these irregular maritime movements of Rohingya Muslims, in cooperation with relevant United Nations agencies, and to ensure that the corresponding burdens and responsibilities are shared at the international level, in particular by States parties to the 1951 Convention relating to the Status of Refugees;

33. Calls upon Myanmar to address effectively the root causes of human rights violations and abuses against ethnic minorities in the Rakhine State, including the Rohingya, and to create the necessary conditions for the voluntary, sustainable, safe and dignified return of all refugees, including Rohingya Muslim refugees, bearing in mind, in particular, that not a single Rohingya has so far returned to the country under the bilateral repatriation mechanism established by Bangladesh and Myanmar, owing to Myanmar's inability to create such conditions in the Rakhine State;

34. Calls upon the international community, in a spirit of genuine interdependence and equitable burden-sharing, to continue to assist Bangladesh in providing humanitarian assistance to Rohingya Muslims and members of other minorities forcibly displaced until they return to Myanmar, and to assist Myanmar in providing humanitarian assistance to all affected persons belonging to an internally displaced community, including in the Rakhine State, taking into account the vulnerability of women, children, the elderly and persons with disabilities;
35. Calls upon all enterprises, including transnational corporations in Myanmar and local enterprises, to implement the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights;

36. Requests the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights to monitor and monitor the implementation of the recommendations made by the independent international fact-finding mission, including those relating to accountability, and to continue to monitor progress made in the field of human rights in Myanmar, including with regard to Rohingya Muslims and other minorities, with the assistance of experts and in addition to the work of the Independent Inquiry Mechanism for Myanmar and the reports of the Special Rapporteur, to submit orally updated information to the General Assembly at its fiftieth and fifth sessions and a report to the General Assembly at its fifty-sixth sessions, each of which is followed by a dialogue, and to submit a report to the General Assembly at its seventy-eighth session;

37. Decides to hold a panel discussion at its fifty-third session on the necessary measures to find lasting solutions to the Rohingya crisis and to end all forms of human rights violations and abuses committed against Rohingya Muslims and other minorities in Myanmar, and requests the High Commissioner to submit a report on the panel discussion to the Human Rights Council at its fifty-fifth session;

38. Calls upon the relevant organs of the United Nations to continue to make concrete recommendations on measures to be taken to resolve the humanitarian crisis, to promote the safe and dignified, sustainable and voluntary return of Rohingya refugees and forcibly displaced persons, and to ensure that perpetrators of mass atrocities, human rights violations and violations of those rights are held accountable;
39. Decides to remain seized of the matter on the basis, inter alia, of the reports of the relevant United Nations mechanisms.

text_fr: [Adoptée sans vote.]
